coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesis and electrochemical corrosion behavior of plasma electrolytic oxidation coating containing an osteoporosis drug on AZ31 magnesium alloy
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmati, M.
Mohammadi Zahrani, E.
Atapour, M.
Noorbakhsh Nezhad, A.H.
Hakimizad, A.
Alfantazi, A.M.
coredata.dc:description: In-situ synthesis of a pure, homogenous coating containing osteofos drug on an AZ31 Mg alloy substrate was reported via a one-step plasma electrolytic oxidation process in a phosphate-based electrolyte using a soft sparking waveform. FESEM/EDX, GIXRD/XRD, ATR/FTIR, XPS, AFM, ICP/OES, static contact angle measurement, OCP, and EIS characterization techniques were used. The coating demonstrated enhanced hydrophilicity and crystallinity, improved general corrosion resistance, reduced porosity, compact morphology, and decreased roughness. AFM technique revealed more peaks than valleys and a more symmetrical height distribution in the drug-loaded coating. XPS technique identified the N 1s (∼401 eV) peak consisting of M − NH2 (∼397 eV), M − N (∼399 eV), and P–N (∼402 eV) bonds confirming the formation of a bisphosphonate/alendronate monolayer. Alendronate molecules were chemically bonded to the coating's outermost layer through –NH2 amino and P–O–H polar heads. Due to the coatings' dual-layer structure, an equivalent electric circuit model with two parallel time-constants simulated the EIS profiles up to 72 h of immersion in simulated body fluid. An inductive loop in the low-frequency end of Nyquist plots indicated sustained drug release and progressive biodegradation within 48 h and 72 h of immersion. Kinetic control was the rate-controlling step in the electrochemical interface.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was conducted when the first author was a postdoctoral fellow researcher under Dr. Ehsan Mohammadi Zahrani's supervision (Contract No. FUM-2102 ) in the Department of Materials Engineering at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad (FUM) . Dr. Maryam Rahmati was partially supported by a grant from the Ferdowsi University of Mashhad (No. FUM-57201 ). The co-author (Amir Hossein Noorbakhsh Nezhad) acknowledged FUM for providing a research assistantship grant (No.FUM- 40968). Plasma Oxide Pars Company was acknowledged for providing PEO apparatus and technical support in surface treatment. Laboratory services provided by the Advanced Corrosion Laboratory in the Materials Engineering Department at the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) were greatly appreciated.

Fig. 12 sets froth Nyquist plots and Bode plots of the total impedance magnitude and phase angle. Fig. 13 exhibits phenomenologically and mathematically rationalized EECs for deconvoluting EIS datasets employing schematic illustrations to visualize the physical significance and interpretation of the EEC models and associated electric parameters. Table 3 lists the calculated model parameters. The Zview software package computes two statistical parameters of χ2 (chi-square) and SS (sum of squares) upon simulating the EIS profiles, establishing the robustness-of-fit (Table 3). As discussed elsewhere, the EEC model's phenomenological rationalization necessitates statistical verification and the physical interpretation of electric circuits [105]. The EEC models' physical justification is supported by cross-sectional FESEM/EDX investigation of the corresponding WEs in Fig. 14. In Fig. 15, FESEM micrographs and elemental composition of different spots on coatings' surface layers upon 24- and 72-hr SBF immersion elaborate on coatings' biodegradability and drug release. The image processing technique determined the porosity fraction and coating thickness from cross-sectional FESEM micrographs (Fig. A2, Appendix A. Supplementary material) after 24 and 72 h immersion times (Table 4), compared to pristine coatings. Table 4 also presents [Mg+2], [Na+1], and [Ca+2] released from coatings to SBF electrolyte in 24- and 72-h immersions. Ion-release measurements are essential to assess the drug-release capability and biodegradation of CWD coating in prolonged immersions. Fig. A3 (Appendix A. Supplementary material) presented EIS profiles of the AZ31 Mg alloy substrate (uncoated) upon 24-h immersion in SBF for comparison purposes.This research was conducted when the first author was a postdoctoral fellow researcher under Dr. Ehsan Mohammadi Zahrani's supervision (Contract No.FUM-57201) in the Department of Materials Engineering at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad (FUM). Laboratory services provided by the Advanced Corrosion Laboratory in the Materials Engineering Department at the Isfahan University of Technology (IUT) were greatly appreciated.

(a, b) FESEM micrographs showing the morphology of grounded drug tablets at two different magnifications and (c) EDX spectrum and elemental analysis of the drug powder.

FESEM micrographs and EDX elemental analysis of selected spots of (a, b) CND and (d, e) CWD coatings showing surface morphology and different types of micropores plus human-body blood, PBS, and SBF droplets' photographs on (c) CND and (f) CWD coatings and surface contact angles.
X-ray elemental mapping of C, Ca, F, Mg, O, and P elements with corresponding EDX spectra and elemental analysis results on the surfaces of (a) CND and (b) CWD coatings.
Cross-sectional X-ray elemental mapping of C, Ca, F, Mg, O, and P elements with corresponding EDX spectra and elemental analysis results: (a) CND and (b) CWD coatings.
GIXRD patterns of (a) CND and (b) CWD coating, and (c) XRD pattern of ground powder of the drug tablets, with standard diffraction lines of identified phases and corresponding ICDD file numbers.
ATR spectra of (a) CND and (b) CWD coating and (c) FTIR spectrum of the drug revealing functional groups and corresponding chemical bonds.
XPS total-survey scan spectra of (a) CND, (b) CWD, and (c) the drug samples with the valence-band assignment.
XPS high-resolution single peak analysis of N 1s, C 1s, O 1s, and P 2p valence bands: (a, d, g, and j) CND and (b, e, h, and k) CWD coatings and (c, f, i, and l) the drug.

2D and 3D AFM images of (a) CND and (b) CWD coating together with the corresponding height distribution curves and Gaussian fitting lines superimposing the histogram profiles of (c) CND and (d) CWD coating and the tabulated roughness parameters.

(a, d, g) OCP curves and EIS profiles comprising (b, e, h) Nyquist plots and (c, f, i) Bode plots of total impedance magnitude (|Z|) and the phase angle (|θ|) vs frequency for CND and CWD coatings upon 24-, 48-, and 72-hr immersion in SBF.
Physical interpretation of the EECs deconvoluting EIS profiles of CND and CWD coatings upon 24-, 48-, and 72-hr immersion times in SBF.
Cross-sectional FESEM micrographs and EDX elemental analysis of (a–d) CND and (e–h) CWD coatings upon (a, b, e, f) 24- and (c, d, g, h) 72-hr immersion in SBF.

FESEM micrographs and EDX elemental analysis of selected spots of the outermost surface layer in (a–d) CND and (e–h) CWD coatings upon (a, b, e, f) 24- and (c, d, g, h) 72-hr immersion in SBF, depicting surface morphology and chemical analysis of accumulated corrosion products.

head.ce:title: In situ synthesis and electrochemical corrosion behavior of plasma electrolytic oxidation coating containing an osteoporosis drug on AZ31 magnesium alloy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In-situ synthesis of a pure, homogenous coating containing osteofos drug on an AZ31 Mg alloy substrate was reported via a one-step plasma electrolytic oxidation process in a phosphate-based electrolyte using a soft sparking waveform. FESEM/EDX, GIXRD/XRD, ATR/FTIR, XPS, AFM, ICP/OES, static contact angle measurement, OCP, and EIS characterization techniques were used. The coating demonstrated enhanced hydrophilicity and crystallinity, improved general corrosion resistance, reduced porosity, compact morphology, and decreased roughness. AFM technique revealed more peaks than valleys and a more symmetrical height distribution in the drug-loaded coating. XPS technique identified the N 1s (∼401 eV) peak consisting of M − NH2 (∼397 eV), M − N (∼399 eV), and P–N (∼402 eV) bonds confirming the formation of a bisphosphonate/alendronate monolayer. Alendronate molecules were chemically bonded to the coating's outermost layer through –NH2 amino and P–O–H polar heads. Due to the coatings' dual-layer structure, an equivalent electric circuit model with two parallel time-constants simulated the EIS profiles up to 72 h of immersion in simulated body fluid. An inductive loop in the low-frequency end of Nyquist plots indicated sustained drug release and progressive biodegradation within 48 h and 72 h of immersion. Kinetic control was the rate-controlling step in the electrochemical interface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw data required to reproduce these findings were already included in the present paper and the Supporting Information file (Appendix A).

ce:section.ce:para: The osteofos drug tablets contain alendronate molecules used for osteoporosis treatment, although the low oral bioavailability of less than 1 % is the major drawback [1–3]. Drug carriers such as biodegradable medical implants can enhance osteoporosis medication's effectiveness by improving drug bioavailability [4–6]. Metallic prostheses made of Ti and its alloys, Co–Cr alloys, and austenitic stainless steels are suitable for orthopedic applications [7,8]. Those alloys are prone to releasing harmful ions or metal debris through corrosion or wear processes, leading to inflammatory diseases and cell cytotoxicity, i.e., reduced biocompatibility.

Magnesium-based metals for biomedical applications have attracted immense attention due to their unique biodegradation properties and biocompatibility. Magnesium is an essential element for human body metabolism, and its mechanical properties closely match those of natural bone tissue, reducing the stress-shielding effect. The biodegradability of magnesium implants also eliminates surgery for implant removal [9,10]. In essence, the corrosion performance of magnesium in the physiological media is a limiting factor attributed to its low standard electrode potential and the unprotective nature of its native oxide/hydroxide film. Chloride ions that typically exist in biological fluids further diminish the corrosion resistance of magnesium and its alloys. Corrosion of magnesium alloys can lead to premature loss of mechanical integrity, local alkalisation, and hydrogen gas accumulation [11]. Surface treatment techniques such as anodizing [12], atmospheric plasma spraying [13], plasma vapor deposition [14], high-pressure cold spraying [15], chemical/electrochemical conversion coatings [16,17], and plasma electrolytic oxidation [11,18] are among various developed methods to enhance magnesium's corrosion resistance [17]. The plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO) technique creates a porous ceramic oxide coating on magnesium alloys by applying high voltages in an alkaline electrolyte. The PEO coatings exhibit superior stability, hardness, adhesion, corrosion performance, wear resistance, and biocompatibility [19]. The PEO coating can also act as a drug career, although excessive porosity diminishes the coatings' mechanical properties and durability. Therefore, pore size distribution control and subsequent pore sealing are essential for the longevity of PEO coatings [20].

Table A1 (Appendix A Supplementary material) presents a literature review on developing bioimplants as a drug career in biomedical applications. Besides PEO, various surface treatment techniques have been recently employed to enhance magnesium bioimplants' capability in drug delivery applications. Using the electrospinning technique, Bakhsheshi-Rad et al. applied a ciprofloxacin drug-loaded gelatin nanofiber coating on an Mg–Ca alloy to enhance its corrosion resistance and antimicrobial properties [21]. Xu et al. utilized the ultrasonic spray process to synthesize composite coatings containing different amounts of poly(d,l-lactic acid-co-ε-caprolactone), poly(ε-caprolactone), and poly(d,l-lactic acid) bioresorbable polymers together with sirolimus drug on AZ31 magnesium alloy, resulting in improved corrosion resistance and controlled drug release [22]. Qi et al. reported the electrophoretic synthesis of a composite coating with chitosan gelatin nanospheres and simvastatin drug, which enhanced corrosion resistance and stimulated bone cell growth [23]. Peng et al. applied a bilayer PEO-LDH (i.e., Layered Double Hydroxide) coating via a hydrothermal process to enhance an AZ31 substrate corrosion resistance, improve the adhesion rate of bone marrow stem cells, and prepare a career for releasing the 5-fluorouracil drug [24]. Bordbar et al. investigated a combination of betamethasone drug layer and PEO coating to protect biodegradable AZ91 magnesium alloy against corrosion under a simulated inflammatory condition [25]. It was deduced that the betamethasone entered the microholes and sealed the porosities; thereby the corrosion resistance improved. The coating exhibited an initial drug release followed by sustained prolonged drug delivery [25].

Herein, we reported the PEO surface treatment of AZ31 magnesium alloy using a soft sparking waveform in a phosphate-based bath containing osteofos drug, i.e., an osteoporosis medication. The following properties of the novel osteofos drug-loaded PEO coating were systematically elucidated with different characterization techniques: surface topography (AFM), wettability (static contact angle measurement), microstructure (FESEM), elemental composition (EDX), chemical functional groups (ATR/FTIR), valence bands (XPS), crystalline phases (GIXRD/XRD), ion-release capability/biodegradability (ICP/OES), and electrochemical corrosion behavior in prolonged immersions in simulated body fluid (SBF) up to 72 h (EIS, OCP measurement, post mortem FESEM/EDX analyses).

Schematic illustrations in Fig. 1 showcase the general overview of the experimental procedure, research methodology, and PEO apparatus. The chemical composition of the AZ31 Mg alloy was previously reported [26–29]. PEO working electrodes were disc-shaped samples, 16 mm in diameter and a thickness of 3 mm, the same as our previous reports. The sample preparation procedure was reported elsewhere [26–29]. PEO treatment was conducted in a phosphate-based electrolyte containing laboratory-grade Na3PO4.12H2O (10 g/L), Ca(OH)2 (2 g/L), and KF (2 g/L) chemical compounds dissolved in 3.5 L of deionized water. Commercially available Osteofos-70 tablets were grounded and added to the bath to achieve a drug concentration of 1 g/L. PEO coating without osteofos drug (0 g/L) was also prepared for comparison (control sample). The CND and CWD sample codes throughout the paper referred to the PEO coatings synthesized by adding 0 and 1 g/L of osteofos drug in the bath, respectively. The electrolyte's temperature of 15 ± 1 °C was maintained by placing the electrochemical cell of 316L stainless steel (Fig. 1) in a water bath equipped with a chiller, as mentioned elsewhere [29]. A 10-min surface treatment was performed at 1 kHz using a soft sparking waveform with 10 % and 20 % positive and negative duty cycles, respectively. Applying the soft sparking waveform could grow a PEO coating with fewer defects and a more compact morphology and uniform thickness, as discussed elsewhere [28–30]. The PEO process was conducted at 440 V and 25 V anodic and cathodic potential peaks, respectively. The current density versus time response of AZ31 electrodes was recorded during the PEO treatment to characterize different stages of the process. The optimized PEO procedure parameters were selected according to Rahmati et al. [28,29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEO bath and coatings characterization techniques
ce:section.ce:para: PEO bath characteristics, including pH, conductivity, and the zeta potential, were determined via a pH meter (AZ 827, Metrohm, Switzerland), conductivity meter (SCHOTT GERATE CG 857, Germany), and DLS Zeta potential analysis equipment (SZ-100, Horiba Scientific, USA), respectively. The latter was determined by the Laser Doppler electrophoresis method. Na and P are the two main elements simultaneously present in the chemical precursors of the bath and the osteofos drug. Therefore, the inductively coupled plasma/optical emission spectroscopy technique (ICP/OES, Optima, 7300DV, PerkinElmer, USA) was used to measure those two elements in baths, with and without osteofos drug addition, to assess the degree of drug solubility in the electrolyte.

FESEM/EDX technique (TESCAN MIRA3 LMU, Brno, Czech Republic) characterized morphology and elemental composition at 15 kV accelerating voltage and 1 nA beam current. The image processing technique calculated porosity fraction and thickness. A surface roughness tester (SJ210 Surftest, Mitutoyo, Japan) measured the roughness. An atomic force microscope (AFM) in non-contact mode assessed the topography and roughness of the coatings using a scanning resolution of 256 × 256 pixels and a scan frequency rate of 1 Hz at a 10 × 10 μm magnification. The Gwyddion (2.62) software package was employed to analyze the AFM results. The Skewness parameter was calculated from the AFM results to elucidate surface roughness. GIXRD technique, i.e., grazing-incident beam X-ray diffraction, identified crystalline phases in coatings with a 5° beam incident angle and a time per step of 3 s. Ground powders of osteofos drug tablets were characterized via an X-ray diffraction technique with a 0.05° step size and time per step of 3 s. The same equipment (GIXRD/XRD, XPert Pro MPD, Malvern Panalytical, UK) performed GIXRD and XRD examinations using Ni-filtered CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 40 kV accelerating voltage and 30 mA beam current within 2θ range of 10–80°. The degree of crystallinity was calculated from the diffraction profiles.

Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Bomem MB 100, ABB group, Switzerland) identified the functional groups and chemical bonding of the coatings and the drug in the 400-4000 cm−1 frequency range with 2 cm−1 resolution. The chemical states of existing elements in ground powders of osteofos tablets and the outermost surface of the CND and CWD coating were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, BESTEC EA 10, Germany). Static wetting contact angle (CA) was measured using a Theta Attension optical tensiometer (KSV Instruments Ltd., Finland) equipped with an automatic multi-liquid dispenser, monochromatic cold light source, and accompanying software, utilizing the Young-Laplace drop profile fitting method. For CA measurements, five μL droplets of a human-body blood sample, SBF, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions (pH of 7.4) were placed on at least three locations of coatings surfaces to conduct at least three static surface contact angle measurements. Bohner and Lemaitre reported SBF's characteristics, standard preparation procedure, and chemical composition [31]. All specified non-electrochemical characterizations were conducted at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro immersion tests and electrochemical corrosion investigations in SBF
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine SBF solution that was utilized as an electrolyte in electrochemical corrosion and immersion tests contained Na+1, K+1, Mg+2, Ca+2, and Cl−1 ions at the concentrations of 142, 5, 1.5, 2.5, and 147.8 (mg/L), respectively. In-vitro immersion tests were conducted for 24, 48, and 72 h in 60 ml of SBF solution with a pH of 7.4 ± 0.1 at 37 ± 1 °C to mimic the corrosive medium of human body plasma. The SBF solutions were poured into sealed medical-grade 120 ml liquid storage containers and placed in an electrical bain marie water bath to maintain 37 ± 1 °C solution temperature throughout the immersion test. Electrochemical tests were conducted at 24, 48, and 72 h immersion intervals in the same containers using a three-electrode electrochemical cell arrangement while the stagnant electrolyte was open to the air. The three-electrode cell arrangement consisted of a working electrode (WE: CND and CWD coatings) with a surface area of 3.71 cm2, a counter electrode (CE: platinum plate), and a KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE). A potentiostat/galvanostat (AMETEK PARSTAT 2273, Princeton Applied Research, USA) conducted open circuit potential (OCP) measurements and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests. The OCP and EIS tests were repeated twice (at least) under each examination condition to ensure the robustness and reproducibility of measured electrochemical datasets. Initially, OCP was recorded during the first 600 s to measure a steady-state cell voltage after placing the WE in the electrochemical cell apparatus. Subsequently, EIS was conducted at a frequency range of 100 kHz-100 mHz by applying an AC voltage amplitude of ±10 mV versus steady-state OCP, based on previous reports [26–29]. Rationalized electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) models fitted into the EIS profiles using a Zview software package to deconvolute measured EIS datasets, including Nyquist plots and Bode plots of phase angle and total impedance magnitude. The statistical error for fitting EECs to EIS profiles was less than 5 % in calculating the corresponding model's parameters. The models' physical interpretation was visualized using schematic illustrations that superimposed EECs to the electrodes' electrochemical interfaces. Noorbakhsh-Nezhad et al. emphasized the necessity of statistical verification and the physical justification of an EEC model to verify its robustness in elucidating electrochemical interfacial phenomena [32,33].

A post-mortem FESEM/EDX analysis of corroded coatings was carried out upon 24 and 72-hr immersion in SBF to deduce corrosion mechanisms. Cross-sectional micrographs were analyzed using an image processing technique to quantify the thickness loss of the immersed coatings and porosity fractions. Concentrations of released Mg, Ca, and Na ions from the coatings to SBF were measured via the ICP/OES technique at 25 °C upon 24- and 72-hr immersions to assess the coatings' biodegradability and drug-release capability in vitro.

Visual inspection of the CWD bath revealed the opaque appearance of the electrolyte caused by the dispersion of partially dissolved osteofos drug particles. In contrast, the CND bath contained a transparent electrolyte due to the complete dissolution of chemical precursors (Fig. 1). Table 1 presents the main characteristics of CND and CWD baths, including pH, conductivity, zeta potential, and concentrations of dissolved Na and P elements. The CND bath is more conductive than the CWD bath. The CWD bath zeta potential (−11.6 mV) is a more negative value than that of the CND bath (−3.6 mV) due to released alendronate groups ([C4H12NO7P2]−1) with a negative charge from the osteofos drug [34]. With identical dissolved amounts in CND and CWD baths, the Na3PO4.12H2O precursor supplies constant concentrations of Na (66.61 mg/L) and P (22.16 mg/L) to the two baths. The osteofos drug provides excessive amounts of Na (1.38 mg/L) and P (0.94 mg/L) to the CWD bath, sourcing from the sodium alendronate/bisphosphonate (C4H12NNaO7P2) constituent of the drug. The measured pH values of the two baths are within the same order of magnitude. Structural hydroxyl ions in the released alendronate groups from the drug caused a slight increase in the pH of the CWD bath (pH = 11.85) compared to the CND bath (pH = 11.71). Sodium alendronate, the foremost pharmaceutical ingredient in the osteofos drug tablets, is the third generation of bisphosphonate impeding bone resorption, chemically named as (4-amino-1-hydroxybutylidene) bisphosphonic acid monosodium [35–37]. Based on density functional theory calculations (DFT) and XPS study, Ma et al. reported sodium alendronate's structural formula and optimal chemical configuration and bondings [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current density vs. time response of AZ31 electrode during PEO treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 presents the plot of current density versus time during the PEO treatment. Three consecutive discharge steps are apparent in the plots, as annotated in Fig. 2a and b. In stage I, occurring within the first 30–40 s, the current density sharply rises, followed by a rapid drop. However, the anodic and cathodic potentials do not reach the maximum during the first 30–40 s. According to Rahmati et al., the anodic voltage exceeded the dielectric breakdown potential in stage I, alluding to the localized formation of oxide coatings and the initiation of local micro-arc and sparking [26]. As seen in Fig. 2a and b, the current density for CND is relatively higher than that of CWD in stage I. Tang et al. [39] argued that a positive correlation emerged between PEO electrolyte conductivity and current density values. Therefore, the noticed trend in current density is consistent with the 12.6 and 5.25 mS/cm conductivities of the CND and CWD baths, respectively (Table 1).

In stage II, the anodic (Fig. 2a) and cathodic (Fig. 2b) current densities reach their maxima by ramping up the corresponding voltages to the absolute magnitudes of 440 V and 25 V, respectively. Reportedly, stage II involves three simultaneously occurring phenomena as follows: (a) swift electrochemical formation of an initial insulating uniform oxide layer on the electrode accompanied by a consistent move of fine expeditious sparks over the whole electrode surface, (b) evolution of hydrogen gas and oxygen within the emerging pores in the anodic oxide layer during cathodic and anodic cycles of the waveform respectively, and (c) absorption/incorporation of cations and loading of additives (osteofos drug in CWD bath) into the growing anodic oxide film [40,41]. In stage II, the current density gradually diminishes, particularly after 60 s. Specifically, the mentioned downward trend is more significant in the anodic current density plot (Fig. 2a) than its cathodic counterpart (Fig. 2b), suggesting three phenomena that simultaneously occur. Those phenomena are as follows: (a) the thickening of the coating (i.e., anodic oxide film), (b) repairing/healing defects in formed oxide film during the cathodic pulse, and (c) electrolyte constituents' loading to the growing oxide layer, e.g., osteofos drug. The anodic current oscillations during stage II indicate plasma discharges, which are more significant in the CWD plot. The intensified surface plasma discharges on the CWD electrode cause accelerated local melting of the substrate, creating a dense, less porous drug-loaded coating as supported by measured porosity fractions in Section 3.3.1. The positive contribution of surface plasma discharges to refining PEO coating morphology is consistent with the literature [42,43].

In stage III, the intensive discharges, visually seen as an orange glow with slow movements over the electrode surface, are randomly distributed [44]. During stage III, the current density is almost constant, with scattered oscillations attributed to the non-uniform formation of a porous anodic film with a varied thickness and subsequent breakdown events and physical stirring of the solution [45,46]. Considering the number and intensity of oscillations (stage III in Fig. 2b), the cathodic current oscillations are more predominant in CND than in CWD coating, making the CND coating more susceptible to microcracks/microporosities and an uneven surface topography with varied thickness as confirmed by FESEM micrographs (Section 3.3.1) and AFM results (Section 3.3.6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coatings and the drug's non-electrochemical characteristics

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the morphology and elemental analysis of the osteofos drug. EDX spectrum in Fig. 3c identifies C, O, Na, and P as intrinsic drug elements, proving purity. However, the H element cannot be detected in the EDX spectrum because of its meager atomic number of one. The N concentration is also below the detection limit; thereby, it is not revealed. Nitrogen is the only suitable marker element to confirm the osteofos drug's presence in CWD coating, further clarified in Section 3.3.5.

FESEM micrographs of CND (Fig. 4 a and b) and CWD (Fig. 4d and e) coatings exhibit surface morphology, microcracks, and oxide particles plus open-mouth and semi-/fully-sealed micropores. EDX spectra reveal the chemical composition of different selected spots. Irregular-shaped, open-mouth micropores and scattered oxide particles resulting from different sizes of random discharge channels, sudden anodic film breakdown, gas entrapment in the oxide film, and ejected partially molten substrate could also be affected by electrolyte chemical composition [47,48]. Semi-sealed micropores are more prevalent in the CWD coating (Fig. 4e) than in the CND coating (Fig. 4b) because the drug particles' anodic absorption could partially seal open-mouth micropores. Generally speaking, fully-sealed micropores could also originate from local electrolyte alkalisation adjacent to open-mouth pores that increase interfacial pH during the cathodic half-period (Fig. 2b), according to Wang et al. [49]. An increase in local pH promotes the formation of oxide constituents that repair/seal micropores and microcracks [41,50], as seen in Fig. 4e. Rapid solidification and thermal stress during the PEO treatment could initiate microcracks [51,52], as shown in Fig. 4a.

Based on comprehensive FESEM analyses of different coatings, the prevalence of microcracks in CWD coating (Fig. 4d) is less than that in CND coating (Fig. 4a), mainly due to lower values of current density maxima in stages I and II for CWD coating compared to CND coating. From our standpoint, loaded to CWD coating, the dispersed drug particles partly contribute to sealing the microcracks, causing a denser coating. EDX spots 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 4e are mainly rich in C and O, the two most abundant elements in the osteofos drug, see Fig. 3c. It indicates osteofos drug loading to CWD coating and subsequent partial sealing of micropores and microcracks by the adsorbed drug particles. Concerning CND coating, (Mg, O, F, P)-rich zones seen in EDX spots 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 4b are ascribed to crystalline MgO, MgP2O7, and MgF2 phases, verified by GIXRD patterns in Section 3.3.3. The carbon content in spots 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 4a is not within the EDX detection limit because there is no drug in the CND coating (Table 1).

X-ray elemental maps of C, Ca, F, Mg, O, and P elements display their spatial distributions on the outermost surfaces of CND (Fig. 5 a) and CWD (Fig. 5b) coatings, verifying coatings' purity and homogeneity. The shown areas in Figs. 5b and 4d are purposefully identical to thoroughly compare and contrast different characteristics of the CWD surface layer and provide a comprehensive picture. The EDX spectra in Fig. 5 correspond to the overall chemical composition of surface areas captured by the elemental maps. At a glance, the spatial distribution intensity of carbon element is more significant in CWD (C: 38.81 at.%, Fig. 5b) than in CND (C: 11.24 at.%, Fig. 5a) coating due to the osteofos drug loading into the former. This finding is also consistent with the drug's chemical composition (C: 57.6 at.%, Fig. 3c). The C element map, particularly in Fig. 5b, confirms that carbon spatial distribution is prominent within the superficial micropores consistent with EDX spectra of spots 1 and 2 in Fig. 4e. The higher content of F element in oxide particles protruded from the CND coating is particularly noticeable in Fig. 5a with a more significant F concentration (CND coating: 8.46 at.% F) than Fig. 5b (CWD coating: 4.57 at.% F). Higher current density (Fig. 2) and the occurrence of more surface discharges justify the increased quantity of scattered oxide particles on CND coating (Fig. 4a and b) than on CWD coating (Fig. 4d and e).

Fig. 6 exhibits cross-sectional FESEM images and elemental maps of C, Ca, F, Mg, O, and P together with EDX spectra and elemental analysis results of the CND (Fig. 6a) and CWD (Fig. 6b) coatings in the regions captured by FESEM micrographs. A robust coatings/substrate bonding is confirmed because CND and CWD coatings demonstrate no disbanding, discontinuities, or interfacial cracking. Coatings thicknesses (CWD: 21.1 ± 3.30 μm, CND: 27.3 ± 3.80 μm) and porosity fractions (CWD: 12.1 %, CND: 21.1 %) are calculated from cross-sectional micrographs (Fig. A1, Appendix A. Supplementary material). The dashed lines in Fig. A1 show the PEO coating's inner and outer barrier layers. A non-uniform distribution of micro sparks and current oscillations in stage III (Fig. 2b) stimulate variations in coatings thicknesses (Fig. 6), resulting in a final wavy surface [53–55]. A thicker, more porous CND coating is brought on by the inflated incorporation of electrolyte constituents to the anodic oxide film, mainly Ca and P compounds, and enhanced throwing of the partially molten substrate [53–55]. Cross-sectional X-ray maps show a relatively uniform spatial distribution of Ca, Mg, O, and P elements, verifying coatings homogeneity. Carbon is mainly concentrated in the interior surfaces of porosities in CWD coating (C: 22.15 at.%), consistent with other findings (Figs. 4 and 5). Nechikkattu et al. mentioned the drug's tendency to accumulate in pore walls [56], consistent with our results. Sourcing from KF precursor, F element tends to form crystalline MgF2 phase (Section 3.3.3) in CND (F: 14.95 at. %) and CWD (F: 10.11 at.%) coatings, aligned with previous studies [57,58]. In Fig. 6b, relatively high C (22.15 at.%) and N (1.72 at.%) concentrations convey the drug loading into CWD coating, in agreement with Figs. 4 and 5. As expected, the EDX spectrum identified no nitrogen in the CND coating (Fig. 6a) because no drug was loaded. Nonetheless, a fundamental uncertainty is associated with absolute concentrations of O, N, and C elements measured by the EDX technique. The EDS is a semi-quantitative method with inherent limitations in precisely measuring light elements with <10 atomic numbers, e.g., O, N, and C.

Cheng et al. proposed a growth model identifying five types of discharges during sparking, including A- and C-type sparks [59]. Surface micropores, oxide granules, and microcracks are mainly caused by A- and C-type interfacial discharges/sparks combined with thermal stresses [59], conveniently occurring in baths with enhanced current density and conductivity, e.g., the CND bath. In precis, the A-type discharge occurs on the coating's outermost interface with electrolyte or gas bubbles that evolved at the coating/electrolyte interfaces [59]. The C-type discharge simultaneously arises within the interior surfaces of open pores and cracks exposed to PEO electrolytes. The predominance of A- and C-type sparks facilitates the incorporation of electrolyte constituents into the CND coating, commonly prevalent in soft sparking waveforms [60]. Accordingly, higher concentrations of Ca and P and the increased thickness in CND coating compared to CWD coating could be justified.

Measured by the surface roughness tester, surface roughnesses of 0.08 ± 0.01, 1.64 ± 0.05, and 1.84 ± 0.30 μm belong to pristine WEs (AZ31 electrodes), CWD, and CND coatings, respectively. Osteofos drug decreases the CWD roughness due to the drug's sealing effect and contribution to tuning the substrate's response to current density (Section 3.2). The CND coating's inflated porosity fraction and its increased cracking susceptibility cause a more statistically significant standard deviation in its average roughness (±0.30 μm) compared to that of the CWD coating (±0.05 μm). Concisely, four factors refined the CWD surface morphology and eliminated porosities and microcracks: (a) decreased bath conductivity, (b) fewer cathodic current oscillations in stage III, (c) reduced sparking intensity, and (4) lower current density (stage II). In contrast, a rougher CND surface emerged due to the rising current density/sparking intensity (stages I and II), improved bath conductivity, and numerous cathodic current oscillations in stage III. Those correlations are consistent with Blawert et al. [53]. Surface roughness of 1–5 μm is commonly believed to foster protein adsorption, collagen synthesis, and osteoblast maturation on bioimplants [61].

Considering the arguments above, the following mechanism is proposed for in-situ, one-step osteofos drug loading into the CWD coating by PEO treatment. On the one hand, during the anodic half-cycle, wherein a positive anodic overpotential emerges under an applied interfacial electrical field, alendronate/bisphosphonate anions, and dispersed drug particles with a negative zeta potential [34] are attracted/absorbed to the electrochemical interface, i.e., a growing anodic oxide film. On the other hand, electrophoretic absorptions of dispersed drug particles and other components with positive zeta potential and cations promote the Ca+2 and C attraction towards the electrode interface, where a negative cathodic interfacial overpotential emerges. Therefore, drug loading occurs in both anodic and cathodic half-cycles.

As a surface energy indicator, the biomaterial's surface wettability is a critical parameter influencing physiologic performance in the human body's biological system [62–64]. Junkar stated that the surface energy and wettability of coatings could be tailored by surface chemistry, surface topography, roughness, and degree of crystallinity [65]. Loading osteofos drug into CWD coating alters those mentioned influential surface parameters, leading to tunning wettability. Eq. Fig. 4c and f illustrate photographs of blood, PBS, and SBF droplets and measured apparent contact angles (θ) associated with CND and CWD coatings, respectively. Geometrically, a low static wetting angle (θ < 90°) causes the liquid droplet to spread well on a solid surface, namely hydrophilic behavior [67]. On the other hand, poor spreading of the liquid droplet on a hydrophobic surface, i.e., a low or non-wetting surface, creates a high wetting angle (θ > 90°) [68,69]. Eq. The static contact angle measurement is selected when the adhesional wetting mechanism is predominant [70]. The following trend in the measured apparent wetting angles is deducible (Fig. 4c and f): θPBS (CND) > θSBF (CND)> θBlood (CND) > 90° > θPBS (CWD) > θSBF (CWD) > θBlood (CWD) > 50°. Overall, hydrophilicity behavior dominates CWD coating, particularly in contact with blood, causing better spreading of the droplets. Meanwhile, CND coating tends to demonstrate a hydrophobic behavior, considering the variations in the apparent contact angles indicated by standard deviation values. From our standpoint, a decreased surface wettability authenticates successful drug loading into CWD coating and surface functionalization by alendronate groups, boosting the coating's hydrophilicity. The enhanced CWD coating hydrophilicity is due to attractive forces between liquid droplets and negatively charged alendronate functional groups with O–H, P–O–H, and NH2 bonds that functionalized the outermost surface layer [71], as confirmed by XPS (Section 3.3.5). Reportedly, terminal functional groups in the outermost surface layer's interface with a liquid droplet dictate surface wettability and polarity [71,72]. Generally speaking, static water contact angles within 0°–62° are believed to expedite osteointegration in titanium bioimplants [34,71,73]. The static wetting angles of alendronate-functionalized CWD coatings (Fig. 4f) are almost within the favorable contact angle range specified in the literature for bioimplants.

The scientific/operating principles of GIXRD and XRD techniques are comparable [74]. However, in contrast to XRD, the X-ray beam's relatively lower incident angle makes the GIXRD technique more precise in characterizing coatings with a few to several micrometers in thickness [75]. In contrast, XRD is more accurate than GIXRD in the phase analysis of powders or bulk materials because of a wide range of X-ray beam incident angles [74,75]. Fig. 7 depicts GIXRD patterns of CND and CWD coating plus the XRD pattern of osteofos drug powder. Fig. 7c identifies sodium alendronate as an existing crystalline phase in the drug tablets, consistent with a previous report [2].

The polycrystalline nature of CND and CWD coatings is apparent in GIXRD patterns containing amorphous and crystalline phases. As annotated in Fig. 7a and b, the prominent diffraction peaks belong to Mg (PDF# 96-151-2520) and MgO (PDF# 96-901-3199) because PEO treatment primarily creates a magnesium oxide layer on the AZ31 substrate [76]. Other identifiable crystalline phases in CND and CWD coatings are MgP2O7 (PDF# 96-153-0319) and MgF2 (PDF# 96-900-9076). The MgF2 formation is consistent with Stojadinovi et al. [77]. A thicker grown anodic layer on the substrate intensifies the MgO and MgP2O7 characteristic peaks in CND coating (Fig. 7a) compared to CWD coating (Fig. 7b), correlating with discussed FESEM/EDX findings in Section 3.3.1 . In addition, more substantial MgF2 peaks appear in Fig. 7a (CND coating), correlating with the F element concentrations reported in Fig. 6a (CND coating: 14.95 at.% F) and Fig. 6b (CWD coating: 10.11 at.% F). As seen in Fig. 7b, minor diffraction peaks of sodium alendronate (PDF# 96-200-5354) emerge in the 2θ range of 10-28o, confirming the drug loading into CWD coating. The low intensity of the drug peaks in Fig. 7b is because of the amorphous nature of the loaded drug or the drug presence in micropores and microcracks within the outermost surface layer, supported by Figs. 4–6. Consequently, due to immoderate X-ray beam penetration depth, the GIXRD technique cannot precisely trace the loaded osteofos drug to CWD coating. However, minor signals of the sodium alendronate are traceable in Fig. 7b.

The Xc of osteofos drug powder, CWD, and CND coatings equal 68.0 %, 62.5 %, and 60.1 %, respectively, signifying the emergence of amorphous phases. Remarkably, no Ca- and Na-containing crystalline phases emerge in the diffraction patterns. The CND and CWD coatings contain Ca element (CND coating: 1.36 and CWD coating: 1.08 at.% Ca, Fig. 6) sourcing from Ca(OH)2 precursor and an insignificant amount of Na element (CND coating: 0.81 and CWD coating: 0.75 at.% Na, Fig. 6) introduced by Na3PO4.12H2O precursor and the drug (just for CWD coating). Due to rapid cooling, amorphous phases could emerge in PEO coatings, which are not detectable by the GIXRD/XRD technique [27,81,82]. Plausibly, Ca-and Na-containing compounds likely emerge as amorphous phases in the coatings, or their amounts are below the detection limit since the GIXRD technique did not reveal those phases. Toulabifard et al. [27] reported the tendency of phosphate-containing compounds to form amorphous phases in the PEO treatment of AZ31 alloy in a phosphate-based electrolyte containing Na3PO4 precursor.

ATR spectra of CND (Fig. 8 a) and CWD (Fig. 8b) coatings and FTIR spectrum of osteofos drug (Fig. 8c) present functional groups and corresponding chemical bonds. The ATR/FTIR band assignments are also annotated. The stretching vibration of MgO in CND and CWD coatings agrees with the GIXRD findings and is consistent with other reports [25,83]. The peaks emerging at 1595 and 879 cm−1 frequencies correspond to the tetrahedral PO4 3− group stretching vibration [84–86]. The PO4 3− bonds are identified in CND and CWD coatings but not in the drug. The P element is mainly incorporated into CND (10.13 at.% P) and CWD (6.27 at.% P, Fig. 6) coatings from Na3PO4.12H2O precursor because the drug contains a minor amount of phosphor, Fig. 3. The PO4 3− peak in CND coating is more intensified than that of CWD coating, in agreement with GIXRD (Fig. 7) and EDX (Figs. 5 and 6) findings. At 810 and 1368 cm−1 frequencies [76], the antisymmetric CO3 2− band could be attributed to atmospheric carbon dioxide absorption [38,87,88]. In all the spectra, the hydroxyl bands at 3531 cm−1 and 3496 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of adsorbed free water molecules or adsorbed –OH groups and intermolecular hydrogen bonds [25,56,88–90]. The stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups of Mg(OH)2 emerges at 3673 cm−1 [76,88], which is more intensified in CWD coating than in CND coating. The amount of Mg(OH)2 phase is expected to be negligible because it is not traceable by the GIXRD technique.

The FTIR spectrum of the osteofos drug consists of several functional groups assigned to O–H (3539–3251 cm−1), C–H (2920 cm−1), C=O (1732 cm−1), N–H (1558 cm−1), C–C (1259 cm−1), C–N (1056 cm−1), and O–H–O (1649 cm−1) bands [38,56,91,92]. The drug's characteristic N–H and O–H–O bands belong to the sodium alendronate compound [38,87,92]. The N–H band is attributed to amino groups (-NH2) [90], correlating with XPS results (Section 3.3.5). The N–H and O–H–O bands emerge in the CWD coating adjacent to the PO4 3− band (Fig. 8b), slightly shifting the PO4 3− peak to higher frequencies. According to Ma et al., the replacement of inorganic phosphate (PO4 3−) groups with alendronate's phosphonic acid groups [C-PO(OH)2] shifts PO4 3− IR band frequency [38]. The N–H and O–H–O bands disappear in the CND coating because no drug is loaded. The drug loading intensifies the C–N band in CWD coating.

XPS investigation further corroborates the in-situ formation of alendronate-functionalized CWD coating. It provides the outermost coating surface layer's chemical composition and electronic state. The EDX, ATR/FTIR, and GIXRD/XRD techniques collect data at areas within a depth of several microns underneath the outermost coating layer. Thus, inherent uncertainties are inevitable when tracing alendronate groups in CWD coating with those techniques because the drug tends to accumulate in superficial micropores (Figs. 5 and 6). The semi-quantitative EDS with a penetration depth of about a few micrometers cannot precisely trace the N element because of its low atomic number [93]. The ICP/OES technique can also not measure the N element [94–96]. According to Fig. 7, the GIXRD/XRD technique, with a penetration depth of several microns [95,96], has inherent limitations in revealing the osteofos drug, particularly its amorphous compounds. XPS technique can accurately reveal the N element of alendronate groups in either crystalline or amorphous states within a 5–10 nm depth in the surface layer [97,98], where the drug is accumulated in superficial micropores.

XPS data consists of total-survey scan spectra (Fig. 9 a–c) and high-resolution single peak analyses of N 1s (∼401 eV), C 1s (∼285 eV), O 1s (∼532 eV), and P 2p (∼134 eV) emissions (Fig. 10 a-l). Table 2 provides valence-band assignments and compositional analysis of deconvoluted high-resolution XPS peaks. Although no signs of Ca-, Na-, and K-containing phases appear in GIXRD and ATR/FTIR spectra, the Ca 2s, Ca 2p [92,98,99], K 2s, and Na 1s [98] valence bands reveal their existence in CND and CWD coatings. As a PEO bath ingredient, residual KF on the coatings surfaces accords with the K 2s and F 1s peaks. The peaks corresponding to Mg KLL [99–101], Mg 2p [99–101], Mg 2s [99–101], F 1s, P 2s, and O 2s core levels correlate with the MgO, MgF2, and MgP2O7 phases divulged in Fig. 7a and b. The peak of 351.9 eV corresponds to overlapping Ca 2P and Mg KLL auger emissions, according to Leśniak-Ziółkowska et al. [99]. The Ca 2p peak emerging at 347 eV binding energy [98] is attributed to Ca+2 in the coatings, alluding to CaO or Ca(OH)2 compounds [99]. The Na 1s peak raising at 1072 eV binding energy results from the PEO bath ingredients, including the osteofos drug and Na3PO4.12H2O compound [98]. XPS spectra revealed that the MgO is the main constituent of the outer layer of grown PEO coating on CND and CWD samples. Most importantly, Fig. 9a–c discloses peaks of N 1s, C 1s [99], O 1s, and P 2p [99] valence bands that favorably fit into a Gaussian-type function, i.e., superimposed solid lines, as showcased in high-resolution single-peak spectra in Fig. 10. The Gaussian-type function fitting is statistically verified, affirming the robustness of the XPS peaks deconvolution. More in-depth analyses of N 1s, C 1s, O 1s, and P 2p high-resolution peaks are indispensable to illuminating alendronate-functionalized surfaces in CWD coating.

In Fig. 10a–c, the symmetric P 2p peak within the binding energy range of 133.32–135.73 eV consists of unresolved P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2 spin-orbit doublet [61,71,92,99]. The P 2p1/2 contribution to the P 2p peak is relatively more significant than P 2p3/2. The P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 peaks could be respectively assigned to the P=O [35] and P–N [86] bonds of alendronate and P–O/P=O bond of phosphate groups [61,102], i.e., MgP2O7, as GIXRD and ATR/FTIR spectra proved. Rojo et al. mentioned P 2p3/2 (134.0 eV) and P 2p1/2 (133.1 eV) as indications of a bisphosphonate monolayer [61]. Notably, the drug loading into CWD coating simultaneously strengthens the P 2p3/2, weakens the P 2p1/2 peak intensity, and slightly shifts them to lower binding energies (Table 2) in CWD coating (Fig. 10b) compared to CND (Fig. 10a). The integral area of the P 2p peak and its relative intensity in CWD coating (Fig. 10b) is relatively larger than CND coating (Fig. 10a) because of emerging phosphorous component of the bisphosphonate groups, substantiating alendronate on the CWD coating's outermost surface layer [92,98].

High-resolution single-peak spectra manifest the O 1s emission deconvolution of CND (Fig. 10j) and CWD (Fig. 10k) coatings and the osteofos drug (Fig. 10l). As seen in Table 2, the binding energy range of 531.05–533.54 eV is ascribed to the O 1s core level region, composed of M − O and O–H (anionic oxygen in hydroxyl) bonds and residual oxygen groups [35,38]. In Fig. 10l, O-H (532.11 eV) and M − O (533.43 eV) are characteristic chemical bonds of alendronate compound [35–38,61]. The M − O and O–H bonds in Fig. 10l (osteofos drug) are attributed to the alendronate groups' P=O and P–O–H characteristic bonds [35,37,71]. In Fig. 10k (CWD coating), the alendronate functional group (a bisphosphonate monolayer [61]) partly contributes to characterized O1 s signals centered at 531.53 eV and 533.54 eV (Table 2), corresponding to H–O–C/H–O–P and P–O–Mg/P=O bonds, respectively [35,61,71]. Overall, in CND (Fig. 10j) and CWD (Fig. 10k) coating, Mg–O, Ca–O/Ca–OH, and Mg–O–P/P=O bonds could concur with the M − O bond because minor peaks of Mg 2s, Mg 2p, Ca 2s and Ca 2p emerge at the same time [35–38,61,99,102]. The M-O-P/P=O bonds emerge in the CND coating (Fig. 10j) because the Mg alloy substrate reacts with phosphate groups in the PEO bath sourced from the Na3PO4.12H2O ingredient but not the drug. The Mg–O contribution to M − O bond intensity (Fig. 10j and k) is more substantial than the other specified compounds because of the potent magnesium oxide content in the coatings, as revealed by GIXRD and ATR spectra. Accordingly, the CND coating's M − O bond is more intensified than that of CWD coating because a higher amount of MgO phase exists in the former, in consonance with GIXRD results (Fig. 7a and b). A stronger O–H bond in CWD coating (Fig. 10k) compared to CND coating (Fig. 10j) is caused by the alendronate groups (P–O–H bond [37]) in the CWD coating, conforming to the O–H–O band in Fig. 8b [35]. To a lesser extent, magnesium hydroxide in CND and CWD coatings could contribute to anionic oxygen in hydroxyl groups (O–H bond) following its minor IR reflection in Fig. 8a and b. However, the Mg(OH)2 contribution to the Mg–O–H bond is insignificant because of its trivial amount in the coatings, indicated by GIXRD spectra (Fig. 7) and a frivolous IR reflection (Fig. 8a and b).

The C 1s peak raises within a binding energy range of 284.20–288.19 eV (Table 2). The four contributions of C 1s emission are C–C (sp2C), C–N, C–O, and C-PO(OH)2 bonds [35,86,102], partly belonging to alendronate functional groups in the osteofos drug. The C 1s spectrum deconvolution in Fig. 10d–f also corroborates ATR/FTIR functional groups (Fig. 8). The phosphate groups supplied by the bath ingredient rather than the drug partly contribute to the C-PO(OH)2 bond in CND and CWD coatings, as we alluded to in the discussion on the P 2p signal. The drug loading to CWD coating (Fig. 10e) raises the C–N and C-PO(OH)2 peak height and decreases the C–C peak intensity compared to CND coating (Fig. 10d), in agreement with ATR results in Fig. 8a and b. The C=C bond does not appear in pure alendronate [86].

Notably, inherent uncertainties are associated with identifying alendronate groups based on P 2p and O 1s photoelectron signals because of interference with phosphate groups supplied by the Na3PO4.12H2O precursor in the PEO bath. The drug is the only C-containing ingredient in the PEO bath (Fig. 3). However, alendronate groups' C 1s photoelectron signal could also interfere with adventitious carbon, leading to an erroneous interpretation. Because of the adventitious carbon interference, Rojo et al. did not recommend the C 1s signal to establish alendronate groups [61]. As an intrinsic elemental constituent of alendronate groups [38], tracing the N 1s photoelectron signal authenticates the osteofos drug/alendronate loading into CWD coating with high accuracy because it does not exist in other bath ingredients. Accordingly, the N 1s signal, as a robust marker of choice to trace alendronate groups [35], is deconvoluted in Fig. 10. The lack of N 1s peak is evident in CND coating, Fig. 10g, in the binding energy range of 395–405 eV because no drug was loaded, consistent with GIXRD findings. Due to the low drug concentration of 1 g/L in the CWD bath, the weak intensity of the N 1s peak is justified, consistent with a previous study [98]. Initially, we examined the N 1s peak fitting with two deconvoluted peaks, which was not promising. Three peaks fitting deconvolution of the N 1s photoelectron signal achieved the best results in agreement with McLeod et al. [98].

In osteofos drug (Fig. 10g) and CWD coating (Fig. 10h), the N 1s peak could be deconvoluted into M − NH2 (∼397 eV), M − N (∼399 eV), and P–N/N–N (∼402 eV) [86] bonds that belong to alendronate groups sourcing from the drug [56,98]. Albano et al. characterized organic bindings of -C-N (399.2 eV) and –NH3 + (401.2 eV) on alendronate-functionalized titanium, attributed to zwitterionic amine structure adsorbed through C-PO(OH)2 groups modulating osteoblast performance [35]. Lee et al. assigned a bond at 399 eV in the alendronate's N 1s photoelectron signal to NH2 because stretching vibrations of N–H amine groups emerged at 3500 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum [86], which is not the case in our research. Mo et al. attributed an isolated single-raised peak at 401.0 eV in the N 1s core level region to alendronate's C–NH2 bond [37]. Zhang et al. ascribed –NH3 +, C–N-, and R–NH2 bonds at binding energies of 401.1 eV, 400.8 eV, and 399.4 eV to N 1s photoelectron signal [90]. Nechikkattu et al. ascribed a positively charged ammonium nitrogen signal at 403.0 eV to N 1s without conducting a high-resolution single peak analysis [56]. The M − N bond could also be attributed to N atoms bonded to Mg in the coating surface layer [71]. As expected, the Mg–N bond is more significant in CWD coating (Fig. 10h) than in the osteofos drug (Fig. 10i). ATR/FTIR spectra (Fig. 8b and c) also subscribe to the N–H bond, agreeing with the NH2 photoelectron signal revealed by XPS. The P–N is the intrinsic bond in the alendronate molecule identified as the most significant contribution to the osteofos drug's N 1s emission (Fig. 10i). Porous nature of CWD coating hinders bisphosphonate detection by XPS [56]. The alendronate adsorption to superficial pore walls (Section 3.3.1) causes a weak N 1s signal in CWD coating (Fig. 10h) compared to the osteofos drug (Fig. 10i).

The emergence of the N 1s photoelectron signal in Fig. 10h further ratified our discussions on O 1s, P 2p, and C 1s peaks, indicating the formation of an alendronate-functionalized surface layer on CWD coating. It could be deduced that alendronate molecules are chemically bonded to the outermost surface of the MgO oxide layer in situ through –NH2 amino and P–O–H polar heads during PEO treatment of the AZ31 substrate. The detection limit of the XPS technique is within the range of 5–10 nm [98]. Notably, there are discrepancies and inconsistencies in the literature assigning different contributing bonds to N 1s, O 1s, C 1s, and P 2P photoelectron signals (particularly N 1s) for alendronate-containing compounds. The lack of consensus is legitimate because of stoichiometry deviations and lattice defects in crystalline/amorphous alendronate complex molecules with different degrees of crystallinity. It could also be affected by other interacting foreign neighbor atoms that differ from one study to another. In particular, the N 1s signal in the discussed literature was a relatively weak peak, making its accurate deconvolution further challenging. The summarised results in Table 2 agree with the most relevant supporting literature and ATR/FTIR findings.

Fig. 11 showcases the 2D and 3D AFM images, roughness distribution curves, and corresponding parameters for CND and CWD coatings. The drug loading to the coating reduces the Zmax value from 1.67 to 1.47 μm, which is consistent with the surface roughness value measured by the roughness tester equipment, as discussed in Section 3.3.1. In Fig. 11c and d, the x-axis corresponds to the Z size, and the y-axis represents the density of peaks. The roughness distribution in CND and CWD coating is non-uniform, as shown in Fig. 11c and d. Therefore, the Gaussian function represented in Eq. (4) deconvoluted the curves using the OriginPro software package.

The negative skewness value of CND coating (−0.6364 a.u.) alludes to more valleys on the surface than peaks (Fig. 11). In contrast, a positive skewness value of 0.05586 a.u. for CWD coating indicates more surface peaks than valleys. The surface has a symmetrical height distribution when the skewness value reaches zero [32]. Therefore, it is plausible to claim that CWD coating possesses a more symmetrical height distribution than the CND coating due to the drug-sealing effect consistent with the FESEM surface micrographs discussed in Section 3.3.1. In addition, the loaded drug to the coating reduces the roughness of the CWD coating more than that of the CND coating, according to the roughness parameters tabulated in Fig. 11.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro immersion and electrochemical corrosion investigations

ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 12a– d, and g, OCP curves correspond to 24-, 48-, and 72-h immersions with annotated steady-state OCP values. Before the EIS test, the OCP curves reach a plateau while no external overpotential is applied (ηa = |ηc| = 0) upon 600 s of immersion in pristine SBF. An insignificant potential drift (<5–10 mV) indicates a steady-state OCP [103,104]. The negative OCP values allude to the working electrodes' tendency to corrosion from a thermodynamic standpoint. The OCPs are about the same magnitude (−1.48 V vs. Ag/AgCl) with negligible variations due to the stable anodic layer on PEO-treated WEs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS study and post-mortem FESEM/EDX and ion-release measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 12 sets froth Nyquist plots and Bode plots of the total impedance magnitude and phase angle. Fig. 13 exhibits phenomenologically and mathematically rationalized EECs for deconvoluting EIS datasets employing schematic illustrations to visualize the physical significance and interpretation of the EEC models and associated electric parameters. Table 3 lists the calculated model parameters. The Zview software package computes two statistical parameters of χ2 (chi-square) and SS (sum of squares) upon simulating the EIS profiles, establishing the robustness-of-fit (Table 3). As discussed elsewhere, the EEC model's phenomenological rationalization necessitates statistical verification and the physical interpretation of electric circuits [105]. The EEC models' physical justification is supported by cross-sectional FESEM/EDX investigation of the corresponding WEs in Fig. 14 . In Fig. 15 , FESEM micrographs and elemental composition of different spots on coatings' surface layers upon 24- and 72-hr SBF immersion elaborate on coatings' biodegradability and drug release. The image processing technique determined the porosity fraction and coating thickness from cross-sectional FESEM micrographs (Fig. A2, Appendix A. Supplementary material) after 24 and 72 h immersion times (Table 4 ), compared to pristine coatings. Table 4 also presents [Mg+2], [Na+1], and [Ca+2] released from coatings to SBF electrolyte in 24- and 72-h immersions. Ion-release measurements are essential to assess the drug-release capability and biodegradation of CWD coating in prolonged immersions. Fig. A3 (Appendix A. Supplementary material) presented EIS profiles of the AZ31 Mg alloy substrate (uncoated) upon 24-h immersion in SBF for comparison purposes.

The deconvoluted EIS profiles signify an electrochemical interface at which the electrode's interfacial Redox reactions are at a thermodynamic equilibrium state at the OCP, close to corrosion conditions of metallic bioimplants in the human body. Immersed coatings in SBF consist of two interfacial sublayers, the inner (dense) and outer (porous) layers [76,89], Fig. 14. The inner layer demonstrates a more compact morphology than the outer layer, typical of PEO coatings [106,107]. Accordingly, the immersed coatings' dual-layer structure correlates with two time-constants shown in EECs (Fig. 13), which is consistent with previous studies [76,89,106–108]. Those two time-constants are also discernible in Nyquist plots (Fig. 12b–e, and h) and Bode plots of phase angle and the total impedance magnitude (Fig. 12c–f, and i). An asymmetric shoulder at higher frequencies and a well-rounded symmetric hump-shaped curve enlarged over medium to low frequencies in phase angle plots correspond to depressed semicircles in Nyquist plots, indicating two time-constants in interfacial electrode phenomena. Considering the shape of EIS profiles, particularly in low frequencies, and the lack of diffusion impedance element in EECs, kinetic control, i.e., activation mechanism, is predominant in Redox reactions [33,109,110]. Orazem and Tribollet established that the Bode plot of phase angle is more responsive than the Nyquist plot to differentiate underlying time-constants that emerged in the electrochemical interface [109]. The number of divisible shoulders or maxima in the Bode plot of phase angle ascertains the minimum number of time-constants [33,105,109]. The physical expounding of EEC models dictates the exact number of interfacial relaxation processes [33,103–105].

Two sets of EEC parameters corresponding to the coating's dual-layer structure (Qout, Rout, and nout plus Qin, Rin, and nin) quantify the outer (out) and inner (in) layers' dielectric properties (Table 3). Qout and Rout represent the outer layer's capacitive behavior and ohmic resistance in contrast to the inner layer's capacitive behavior (Qin) and ohmic resistance (Rin). Those parameters are independent of frequency and correspond to the constant phase element (CPE), statistically enhancing EIS data fitting quality better than a pure capacitance (C) element [33]. The porous coating's sublayers behave as non-ideal capacitors in EIS, necessitating the replacement of pure capacitance with CPE in EEC. The n variable is a dimensionless quantity with unknown phenomenological interpretation (0 < n < 1) [109–112]. Particularly in CWD coating (Table 3), the nin values tend toward 0.5 as immersion time increases, indicating further deviation of the coating's sublayer response from ideal capacitive behavior due to progressive biodegradation and drug release. If n equals 0.5, a Warburg diffusion impedance emerges at the electrochemical interface due to non-uniform mass transfer, indicating corrosive species' diffusion as the rate-controlling step [110–112], improbable in our study as 0.5 < n < 1 (Table 3). The EEC (Fig. 13) describes two superimposed inert porous layers wherein the inner layer is denser than the outer layer, as discussed by Orazem and Tribollet [109], Huang et al. [111], and Lasia [112]. The active nature of the AZ31 Mg alloy substrate underneath the coating's sublayers promotes an inductive impedance response, where corrosive species penetrate through porosites and reach the substrate in prolonged immersions. Our previous work discussed the frequency-independent nature of CPE and its origin, the CPE's physical meaning, and the electrochemical/physical interpretation of CPE's parameters (Q, R, and n) in an electrodeposited porous nickel coating [33]. Noorbakhsh-Nezhad et al. [33] also discussed Brug's equation [113] to calculate the CPE's effective capacitance (Ceff) from Q, R, and n parameters in contrast to a proposed formula by Hsu and Mansfeld [114]. The calculated Ceff (out) and Ceff (in) from Brug's equation are reported in Table 3 and attributed to CPEout and CPEin, respectively. The CPE appears as a depressed semicircle in the Nyquist plot, in essence, accompanied by a steeping hump-shaped curve in phase angle plots wherein the maximum phase angle value is well below 90° [33,109–112]. A 3D time-constant distribution at an electrochemical interface originates CPE in porous electrodes [109,115,116], e.g., CND and CWD coatings.

In CWD (48 h) and CND/CWD (72 h) coatings, an inductive response arises in low frequencies in Nyquist plots, which are introduced to the model by L and RL electric parameters (Table 3) in parallel to the RinQin circuit (Fig. 13). Considering the arisen low-frequency inductive response, the Qin and Rin parameters equal Qdl and Rct because corrosive species reach the substrate through porosities [109–112]. The Rct is charge transfer resistance associated with Redox reactions occurring along the electrochemical double-layer adjacent to the substrate in prolonged immersions. Qdl represents the electrochemical double-layer capacitive behavior at the bottom of micropores and microcracks in the inner layer, considering the non-reactive nature of pore walls [111]. Because the PEO route synthesizes a porous magnesium oxide coating with a non-conductive nature on the substrate, the developed micropores/microcracks in the inner layer in prolonged immersion times (Fig. 14c and g) have non-reactive walls. Arguably, the Redox reactions occur at the bottom of pores/cracks at the emerging electrolyte interface with the substrate wherein the electrochemical double layer exists, schematically illustrated in Fig. 13b. Huang et al. [111] and Lasia [112] discussed interpretation principles for EIS response of porous electrodes and corresponding physical models in detail. In prolonged immersions, the diameter of the larger depressed semicircle expanding over the medium to low frequencies in Nyquist plots (Fig. 12b–e, and h) is proportional to Rct because Qdl and Rct parameters are parallel elements in the EEC model (Fig. 13) [110]. The Rs symbol in Fig. 13 denotes the SBF electrolyte solution resistance between WE and RE [33]. The diminished inner layer's barrier properties induce the coatings' inductive response. It stimulates SBF electrolyte's access to open pores and microcracks to reach the inner layer/substrate interface (Fig. 13). Consequently, Redox reactions take place on locally unprotected Mg substrate, leading to the sudden drop of real impedance part and a positive imaginary impedance component in Nyquist plot's low-frequency end [112,117]. The inductive response appears as a low-frequency hook in Nyquist plots (Fig. 12e and h) of CND (72 h: 1.58–0.1 Hz frequency range) and CWD (48 h: 1.58–0.1 Hz, and 72 h: 2.35–0.1 Hz frequency range) coatings. Fig. 14 evinces the dominance of open micropores and microcracks in the inner layer upon prolonged immersion, promoting inductive behavior. The time-constant corresponding to the outer layer's dielectric properties emerges as a trifling, depressed semicircle in the Nyquist plot and a little asymmetric shoulder in Bode plots of phase angle at high-frequency ranges, alluding to the low ohmic resistance of the outer layer (Rout). The porous outer layer with open micropores and scattered closed pores (Fig. 14) exacerbates weak barrier properties/low ohmic resistance. At each test point in Table 3, the Rout value is relatively more significant in CWD coating than in CND coating, demonstrating a positive correlation with immersion time. The Rout values change in the following order:

A relatively substantial steeping hump-shaped curve raising in the Bode plot of phase angle correlates with a sizeable depressed semicircle extended over high-to low-frequency ranges in the Nyquist plot, assigned to the inner layer's capacitive behavior. In the 24-h test point (Fig. 12c), the most symmetric maxima at 25.11 Hz appear in the Bode plots, indicating the coatings' favorable corrosion resistance. According to Tables 3 and in the absence of an inductive behavior (i.e., CND coating: 24 h and 48 h, CWD coating: 24 h), the coatings' corrosion resistance is more positively influenced by the inner layer rather than the outer layer (Rin ≫> Rout) due to the inner layer's compact morphology (Fig. 14a and e). In 24 h, the best corrosion efficiency belongs to CWD coating because
R
i n

(12.37 kΩ cm2) >

(9.08 kΩ cm2) and

C N D − 24 h r
(62.66°), attributed to the loaded drug's pore-sealing effect, although CND coating (18.6 ± 2.5 μm) is thicker than CWD coating (16.30 ± 1.7 μm), see Table 4. The |θ| values belong to the maxima of hump-shaped curves in Bode plots of phase angle. Post-mortem cross-sectional FESEM micrographs (Fig. 14) and the calculated thickness loss (Table 4) prove that CWD coating's corrosion resistance outperforms its rival upon 24 h of immersion. In 24 h (Table 4), the CWD coating demonstrates a thickness loss of 22.7 %, smaller than the CND coating (31.8 %), implying that the CWD coating's better corrosion performance aligns with EIS findings. Improved corrosion performance in CWD coating is also indicated by higher [Ca+2] released from CND ([Ca+2] = 24.16 mg/L) than CWD ([Ca+2] = 22.18 mg/L) coating to the SBF electrolyte over the first 24 h of immersion (Table 4).

Compared to the pristine condition (Table 4), the porosity fraction of CND coating falls by 24.6 % in 24 h due to the accumulation of corrosion products, which leads to the release of less [Mg+2] to the electrolyte (24 h: [Mg+2]CND = 58.17 < [Mg+2]CWD = 126.0 mg/L). In contrast to Fig. 4a and b, Fig. 15a and b presents the surface morphology of CND coating in 24 h, displaying micropores filled with (Mg, O)-rich corrosion products. In the same period, the porosity fraction of CWD coating jumps by 23.9 %, implying the release of loaded drugs. Compared to Fig. 4d and e, Fig. 15e and f corroborate the drug releasing from CWD coating, considering high concentrations of N and C in the EDX spectrum of spot 3 in Fig. 15f. A significant increase of [Na+1] also indicates sodium alendronate releasing from the osteofos-drug loaded coating (24 h: [Na+1]CWD = 890.5 > [Na+1]CND: 752.3 mg/L). The drug released from CWD coating's porosities prepares more open pores for the SBF electrolyte's penetration toward the substrate, indicated by the release of excessive [Mg+2] sourcing from the AZ31 substrate (24 h: [Mg+2]CWD = 126.0 mg/L > [Mg+2]CND = 58.17). Consequently, an inductive loop appears in the CWD coating's Nyquist plot in 48 h (Fig. 12e), whereas CND coating retains its inner layer's barrier properties up to 72 h.

As immersion time prolongs (i.e., 48 h and 72 h test points), the humps in Bode plots of phase angle (Fig. 12f and i) tend to become more asymmetric in shape and gradually move toward lower frequency and phase angle values. It alludes to accelerated corrosion kinetics [33] and sustained drug release, which could also be interpreted as enhanced biodegradation over prolonged immersions. In 72 h, an inductive loop also emerges in CND coating's Nyquist plot (Fig. 12h), identical to CWD coating. Numerous microcracks appear in the inner layers of the CWD (Fig. 14g) and CND (Fig. 14c) coatings. As immersion time increases from 24 h to 72 h, CWD coating loses its thickness by 10.43 %, about a thickness loss of 8.6 % in CND coating (Table 4). The CND coating releases about the same [Mg+2] to the SBF electrolyte as CWD coating (72 h: [Mg+2]CND = 97.51 vs [Mg+2]CWD = 98.94 mg/L). According to Table 4, the porosity fraction decreases in CND and CWD coating by 25.8 % and 28 % in 72 h compared to 24 h because of corrosion product accumulation in micropores (Figs. 13 and 14).

In Fig. 14f and h, EDX results reveal a decrease in C and N elements (drug's indicators) and a rise in Mg and O elements (corrosion products' indicators, e.g., magnesium hydroxide) from 24 to 72 h, aligned with EDX results in Fig. 15e and g, corresponding to CWD coating. Those elemental changes suggest gradual drug release and accumulation of corrosion products in CWD coating as immersion time prolongs. In Table 4, [Mg+2] drops by 21.5 % from 126.0 mg/L (24 h) to 98.94 mg/L (72 h), confirming corrosion product accumulation in CWD coating micropores that partially seals the CWD coating's inner layer; thus,
R
i n
C W D − 72 h r
(8.02 kΩ cm2) >
R
i n
C N D − 72 h r
(7.63 kΩ cm2). The CND coating, in contrast, demonstrates a 67.6 % jump in [Mg+2], from 58.17 mg/L (24 h) to 97.51 mg/L (72 h), indicating the insignificant sealing effect of corrosion products mainly because of the larger size and fraction of pores in CND coating than in CWD coating (Table 4). Gradual Ca+2 release from CND and CWD coatings over time positively correlates with progressive thickness loss (Table 4). As noted in Table 4, Ceff values for the inner layer of the CWD coating are more significant than those of the CND coating in 48- and 72-h immersion time, which could be attributed to the accumulation of corrosion products, enhancing the effective capacitance. Concisely, CWD coating demonstrates an enhanced corrosion resistance than CND coating in 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h immersion in SBF because the sum of Rin and Rout for CWD coating is more significant than that of CND coating in the examined immersion times (Table 3) even though the CWD coating thickness is less than CND coating. This trend is justifiable because of (a) the sealing effect of the drug solid particles loaded to micropores in CWD coating, (b) the lower porosity fraction of CWD compared to CND coating (Table 4), and (c) the sealing effect of corrosion products, Mg(OH)2 in particular, accumulated in open pores (Fig. 15). Although the loaded drug to CWD coating is gradually released over prolonged immersions in SBF for up to 72 h, emerging corrosion products plug the open pores.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The facile, in-situ synthesis of a pure, homogenous, osteofos-drug-loaded PEO coating on AZ31 Mg alloy substrate through a one-step PEO surface treatment process in a phosphate-based electrolyte using a soft sparking waveform was reported.
The osteoporosis drug loading to the coating was predominant during the anodic half-cycle because the sodium alendronate/bisphosphonate groups possessed a negative zeta potential.

Four factors refined morphology and eliminated porosities and microcracks in the coating containing the osteoporosis drug: (a) decreased electrolyte conductivity, (b) lower current density in stage II, (c) reduced sparking intensity, and (d) fewer cathodic current oscillation in stage III.
The most abundant crystalline phase in the drug-containing coating was MgO with minor amounts of MgP2O7, MgF2, and C4H12NNaO7P2 (sodium alendronate).

The homogeneous coating containing the drug, with robust bonding to the substrate, demonstrated enhanced hydrophilicity, improved crystallinity, and a more compact morphology with decreased roughness due to the sealing effect of the drug. The drug loading also caused the coating's surface topography to have more peaks than valleys with a more symmetrical height distribution.

N 1s (∼401 eV) peak consisting of M − NH2 (∼397 eV), M − N (∼399 eV), and P–N (∼402 eV) bonds together with C 1s (∼285 eV), O 1s (∼532 eV), P 2p (∼134 eV) emissions confirmed a bisphosphonate/alendronate monolayer emerging on coating's outermost layer. ATR spectroscopy characterized N–H (1558 cm−1) and O–H–O (1649 cm−1) bonds in the drug-loaded coating consistent with XPS. Alendronate molecules were chemically bonded in situ to the outermost surface layer through –NH2 amino and P–O–H polar heads.

Attractive forces between liquid droplets and negatively charged alendronate functional groups with O–H, P–O–H, and NH2 bonds fostered hydrophilicity. The following trend emerged in the static wetting angles of PBS, SBF, and human body blood: θPBS (CND) > θSBF (CND)> θBlood (CND) > 90° > θPBS (CWD) > θSBF (CWD) > θBlood (CWD) > 50°.

Due to the coatings' dual-layer structure, an EEC model with two parallel time-constants without a diffusion impedance element simulated EIS profiles up to 72 h of immersion in SBF. An inductive loop emerged in Nyquist plots' low-frequency end in prolonged immersion, indicating sustained drug release and progressive biodegradation within 48 h and 72 h of immersion. Kinetic control/activation polarisation was the rate-controlling step in the electrochemical interface.

The drug-containing coating demonstrated superior resistance to general corrosion due to refined morphology and drug-sealing effect, as confirmed by decreased thickness loss and porosity fraction in prolonged immersions and higher ohmic resistance of the coating's sublayers at each immersion time compared to the control sample.
